--

As React Native applications grow, managing components, screens, and business logic becomes increasingly challenging. What starts as a handful of files can quickly turn into hundreds of components spread across multiple folders. Without a clear structure, maintaining the codebase becomes difficult, especially when multiple developers are working on the same project.
One design methodology that has proven effective for organizing UI components is Atomic Design, often referred to as Atomic Architecture. Originally introduced by Brad Frost for web applications, the concept adapts seamlessly to React Native and helps developers build scalable, reusable, and maintainable mobile applications.
In this article, we’ll explore Atomic Architecture, understand its five building blocks, and learn how to implement it in a React Native project.
What Is Atomic Architecture?
Atomic Architecture is a component-driven design methodology that organizes UI elements into a hierarchy of reusable building blocks. Instead of creating large, monolithic components, the interface is broken down into smaller pieces that can be combined to create more complex screens.
The five levels of Atomic Design are:

• Atoms

• Molecules

• Organisms

• Templates

• Pages
